item.title: Pan-Seared Shrimp with Garlic and Lemon: Technique and Timing
item.description: A straightforward guide to cooking shrimp perfectly every time. Learn why proper pan searing requires dry shrimp, high heat, and minimal crowding, plus the exact doneness cues that prevent overcooking this delicate protein.
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 10 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 25 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 4

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 1.5 lbs (680g) large shrimp (16-20 count per pound), peeled and deveined
  • 3 tbsp (45ml) extra-virgin olive oil, divided
  • 4 cloves (20g) garlic, thinly sliced
  • 1/2 lemon (25g), cut into wedges
  • 1/2 tsp (3g) fine sea salt
  • 1/4 tsp (0.5g) black pepper
  • 1/4 tsp (0.5g) red pepper flakes (optional)
  • 2 tbsp (30g) unsalted butter (optional, for finishing)
  • 2 tbsp (8g) fresh parsley, chopped (optional garnish)

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. 1 Remove the shrimp from the refrigerator 10-12 minutes before cooking. Cold shrimp coming straight from the refrigerator will dramatically cool down your pan when added, dropping the surface temperature by 50-100°F or more. This temperature drop is the enemy of good browning. As the shrimp sits at room temperature, the interior gradually warms while the exterior reaches thermal equilibrium with the air. You'll notice the shrimp become more flexible and pliable as they warm. If your kitchen is cold, give them the full 12 minutes. If it's warm, 10 minutes is sufficient. Check that your shrimp are indeed deveined by running your finger along the back curve—you should feel a clean line without any dark vein running along the back. If you see any remaining dark matter, use a small pointed knife to trace the vein and lift it out. A small amount of residual vein won't harm the final dish, but removing it creates cleaner appearance and slightly better texture. 10-12 minutes Shrimp feel cool but not cold to touch, flesh is flexible and pliable, no condensation visible on surface
  2. 2 Pat the shrimp completely dry using paper towels. This step determines whether your sear will succeed or fail, yet it's the step most people rush. Press gently but firmly on each shrimp's surface, rotating it to dry all sides, especially the underside which tends to hold moisture. This isn't a light dab—this is actual pressure, using multiple paper towels if needed. You'll feel moisture transfer to the towel. Once you think they're dry, do another pass. I typically use three passes of paper towels for a pound and a half of shrimp. Hold a shrimp up to the light and look at its surface—it should appear completely matte, not shiny. Any glistening indicates residual moisture. If moisture remains when the shrimp hits the hot oil, it will create steam that prevents the shrimp from reaching the pan's surface temperature. This forces you to cook longer to compensate, inevitably overcooking the interior. 5-7 minutes Shrimp surface appears completely matte with no shine, no moisture transfers to towel when pressed, shrimp doesn't feel slippery when handled
  3. 3 Place a large skillet (10-12 inches / 25-30 cm) over medium-high heat and add 2 tablespoons (30ml) of extra-virgin olive oil. The pan should be completely dry—any water remaining from washing will cause the oil to splatter when it heats. Wait about 2 minutes before adding shrimp. You'll see the oil's surface become fluid and start to shimmer. When you tilt the pan, the oil should ripple across the entire surface. The oil should not smoke—if it does, your heat is too high and the oil's chemical structure is breaking down, which degrades flavor and creates off-tastes. Medium-high heat is the correct setting. If your stovetop runs hot, you might need to set it between medium and medium-high. The goal is an audible sizzle that's steady and purposeful, not violent or aggressive. 2 minutes Oil shimmers across entire pan surface, small heat waves visible above oil, no smoke present, oil flows freely when pan is tilted
  4. 4 Working in two batches if necessary, arrange shrimp in the hot oil in a single uncrowded layer. Space them so there's approximately a quarter-inch (6mm) of visible pan surface between each shrimp. They should not touch. This spacing is non-negotiable. The space between shrimp allows air circulation and ensures heat reaches all surfaces evenly. More importantly, each shrimp maintains contact with the hot pan surface, not with other shrimp. In crowded pans, shrimp touch each other and essentially steam where they contact. If your pan typically holds eight shrimp comfortably spaced, cook eight at a time. Resist the urge to fit more—it's far better to cook three batches of eight shrimp than to crowd the pan and end up with steamed, pale shrimp. Do not move or flip the shrimp for the first 2 minutes. Resist the urge to check on them. The browning (Maillard reaction) develops on the bottom surface only through sustained direct contact with the hot pan. Moving the shrimp breaks this contact and restarts the process. You should hear a steady, purposeful sizzle. If it's a soft hiss, the pan isn't hot enough. If it's a violent pop, the heat is slightly too high and the oil may be starting to smoke. 2 minutes (first side) Steady audible sizzle, shrimp bottoms develop golden-brown tint visible along edges, shrimp edges begin to curl slightly at the very ends, immediate aroma of searing shrimp and garlic becomes apparent
  5. 5 Flip each shrimp and cook for exactly 1.5 to 2 minutes on the second side. This is the moment where precision matters most. Look at the shrimp's color—it should be opaque and white across approximately 90% of its surface. The very center of the thickest part may still show a barely-visible thin line of slight translucence when you look at the shrimp straight-on. This translucence indicates the interior is still at 140-145°F (60-63°C)—exactly where you want it. If you see the shrimp forming a tight C-shape with the edges curled severely, you've exceeded the target temperature. The proteins have contracted so much that the shrimp is now tough. The bottom side will brown less than the first side because the pan has lost some heat and the exterior is already setting, but this is normal and actually desirable. A shrimp with one golden side and one slightly lighter side indicates perfect timing. Uniform browning on both sides typically indicates slight overcooking. 1.5 to 2 minutes (second side) Shrimp mostly opaque white with opaque center, barely-visible translucence only at the absolute middle when viewed straight-on, sides beginning to curl but not extreme, internal temperature reaches 140-145°F (60-63°C) if measured with an instant-read thermometer
  6. 6 Add the sliced garlic and red pepper flakes (if using) to the pan and stir constantly for 20-30 seconds. This is the only moment when raw garlic goes into the pan, and the timing is critical. If you add garlic at the beginning of cooking alongside the shrimp, the sustained high heat will burn it, creating bitter, acrid flavors. By adding garlic only now, after the shrimp finishes cooking, you're relying on the pan's residual heat—still very hot, but dropping gradually. The garlic's sulfur compounds break down and become mellow and sweet rather than sharp and pungent. The garlic won't color or brown visibly; it will remain pale gold or white and release a gentle, fragrant aroma. 20-30 seconds is the perfect window. At 15 seconds, the garlic won't be flavorful enough. At 60 seconds, it starts to brown. I often remove the pan from heat immediately after the shrimp finishes and add the garlic, then count to 25 slowly. This gives me a consistent result every time. 20-30 seconds Garlic becomes fragrant and slightly softened, no browning visible, aroma shifts from harsh to mellow and sweet
  7. 7 Remove the pan from heat immediately. This is perhaps the most important sentence in these instructions. Remove the pan. From the heat. Now. The residual heat in that pan is still high enough to continue cooking the shrimp through carryover heat—the temperature won't immediately drop just because you've removed it from the flame. The pan remains hot for several minutes. If the shrimp sit in this hot pan for even 30 seconds longer, the proteins continue tightening and squeeze out moisture. Your perfect shrimp becomes noticeably tougher. Squeeze the lemon wedges over the shrimp and garlic. The fresh lemon juice brightens the shrimp's natural brininess and sweetness, preventing the dish from tasting flat or one-dimensional. If using the optional butter, add it now. The residual heat will melt the butter into the oil and lemon juice, creating a light sauce that's silky and luxurious without overwhelming the delicate shrimp flavor. Toss everything together very gently for 10-15 seconds to combine. Gentle is the operative word—rough handling can break apart the just-cooked shrimp. 1 minute Lemon juice distributed throughout pan, butter (if using) melted and incorporated, aroma becomes bright and citrus-forward, shrimp glisten from oil and lemon
  8. 8 Transfer the shrimp immediately to a serving plate or bowl. Speed matters here. Warm plates are better than room-temperature plates because they slow the cooling process, keeping the shrimp at optimal serving temperature longer. If serving to guests, warm the plates by running hot water over them while cooking, then dry them thoroughly. Arrange the shrimp on the plate immediately. Serving shrimp family-style in a shared bowl works beautifully and feels generous. Garnish with fresh chopped parsley if desired. The green color adds visual interest and the parsley's fresh flavor complements the shrimp and lemon. Taste a piece and assess the seasoning. The combination of lemon and the natural brininess of shrimp usually requires minimal additional salt—I rarely add more than a pinch. If the shrimp tastes flat or one-dimensional despite the lemon, add a light sprinkle of fleur de sel or Maldon salt (which has a different crystal structure and dissolves on the tongue rather than making the shrimp taste salted). The dish tastes best served immediately while the shrimp is still warm and the pan sauce is still fluid. Once it cools, the oil solidifies slightly and the texture becomes less appealing. 1 minute Shrimp transferred to warm serving plate, still steaming slightly, pan sauce coating the pieces, parsley visible as garnish, shrimp glistens from oil and lemon

media:content.media:description: A straightforward guide to cooking shrimp perfectly every time. Learn why proper pan searing requires dry shrimp, high heat, and minimal crowding, plus the exact doneness cues that prevent overcooking this delicate protein.
